Q1: I tried to keep a consistent schedule where I'd set aside time on weekends and a couple evenings to study. I'd print out readings and make handwritten notes since that's how I learn best. The routine was there in theory, but honestly, I struggled to actually follow through most weeks. I'd sit down with my materials but then feel overwhelmed and end up just going through the motions without really absorbing much.
Q2: I really wanted to be someone who studies at regular times, and I'd plan out my week carefully. But my pattern ended up being pretty inconsistent despite my intentions. When deadlines approached, I'd panic and try to cram, but even then I couldn't always make myself focus. Life stuff and my part-time job made it hard to stick to any real schedule.
Q3: Honestly, I only logged in when I absolutely had to check something specific, like a deadline or an announcement. Sometimes I'd log in because I felt guilty about not being more active. It wasn't really part of my natural workflow since I preferred working with printed materials. I'd go weeks without logging in and then feel scared about how behind I was.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried to make it a habit at the beginning of the semester, but it never stuck. I found the platform kind of overwhelming with all the different courses and materials. I ended up just downloading what I needed and working offline, so the LMS never became something I naturally turned to.
Q5: The downloadable readings and PDFs were most useful because I could print them out and annotate them by hand. I learn best through reading and writing, so having those text-based resources was essential. The videos were okay too, I watched quite a few when I was trying to catch up, but reading always felt more productive for me.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion boards. I just couldn't bring myself to post anything publicly, it felt too exposed and I was scared of saying something wrong. I also barely touched the interactive quizzes and practice activities because they stressed me out. If something wasn't strictly required, I usually skipped it to focus on what seemed most essential.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to what I'd be tested on. I needed to feel like I was making progress toward something concrete. If it was just optional enrichment material, I couldn't justify spending time on it when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q8: Yes, they felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them that made me anxious but also motivated me to at least attempt them. Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was barely managing the mandatory stuff. I'd tell myself I'd come back to them later, but I never did.
Q9: Almost all of my real studying happened offline. I'd download readings, print them out, and study from my notes in the library or at home. I have limited internet at home since money is tight, so working offline was practical too. My notebooks were full of summaries and annotations that the LMS would never know about.
Q10: There were many times I spent hours going through printed readings and rewriting notes by hand, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also study with classmates in person, quizzing each other from our notes. My real engagement happened away from the screen, in my notebooks and through conversations with peers.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a PDF, maybe skim it once online, and then print it to actually study. The LMS would show one quick access, but I'd spend hours with that same document in paper form. This was my standard approach for almost every reading in the course.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. I was scared about my grades, working extra shifts to pay rent, and feeling like I was drowning. My attendance dropped because I was exhausted and anxious about facing how behind I was. The fear kind of paralyzed me instead of motivating me to catch up.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS felt like just another overwhelming thing to deal with. I'd avoid logging in because seeing all the incomplete tasks made my anxiety spike. Sometimes I was too mentally drained to do anything productive even when I had the time.
Q14: Yes, many times. I'd watch videos or scroll through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I was going through the motions because I felt like I should be doing something, but nothing was actually sinking in. Those sessions felt pointless but I didn't know how to make myself focus better.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The fear of doing them badly made me avoid starting them at all. It's like I was scared to confirm that I didn't understand the material, so I just... didn't try. By the time I'd work up the courage, it was often too late.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that really pushed me to log in and submit anything. I'd have this burst of panicked activity right before something was due. But even then, I often missed deadlines because my fear of failure made me freeze up. The deadline pressure was more paralyzing than motivating for me.
Q17: I barely got any feedback because I barely submitted anything. When I did get feedback, it sometimes made me feel worse because it highlighted how much I was struggling. I wanted feedback to help me improve, but by the time I'd get it, I was already too far behind to apply it meaningfully.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many modules and activities spread across 19 online courses. I couldn't figure out what to prioritize, so I'd just pick things randomly or focus on whatever seemed least scary. A clearer, more linear structure might have helped me stay on track better.
Q19: Having so many resources available actually made engagement harder for me. I didn't know where to start or what was most important. The flexibility that's supposed to help students just left me feeling lost and anxious. Simpler, more guided pathways would have worked better for someone like me.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I'd log in, watch videos, browse materials, but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The fear of being judged or failing made the final step of submission terrifying. Activity doesn't equal productivity when anxiety is blocking you from completing things.
Q21: That's not my experience at all, unfortunately. My low activity matched my low performance. But I can imagine students who are efficient learners, who study smart offline and just submit their work without needing to spend hours on the platform. I wish I could be like that.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the printed notes, the study groups. They definitely can't capture the fear and anxiety that might be preventing someone from engaging even when they want to.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just clicking through things or being physically present. For me, engagement also means feeling connected to the course and believing you can succeed, which I struggled with a lot.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was all semester, how much I wanted to do well but felt paralyzed by fear of failure. It wouldn't show my financial stress or how exhausted I was from working. It also wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed readings and handwritten notes, genuinely trying to learn even when I couldn't bring myself to submit anything.
